symbols: Dove descending — the Holy Spirit manifesting at Christ's baptism
God the Father in the clouds — the voice declaring 'This is my beloved Son'
Water of the Jordan — baptismal waters sanctified by Christ's immersion
John pouring water — the act of baptism by the forerunner
Donor kneeling in prayer — the faithful believer present through devotion at the sacred event
description: Christ stands nearly nude in the center of the Jordan river, hands clasped in prayer. John the Baptist in a red robe pours water over Christ's head from the right. On the left, a richly dressed donor figure kneels in elaborate brocade vestments. Above, a golden dove descends toward Christ, and at the top of the painting, God the Father appears in a radiant glory surrounded by angels. A lush landscape with trees, hills, and distant figures extends behind the scene.
theological_description: The Baptism of Christ is the preeminent Trinitarian theophany in the Gospels. All three Persons of the Trinity are simultaneously manifest: the Son in the water, the Spirit descending as a dove, and the Father's voice from heaven: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased' (MAT.3.17). John the Baptist fulfills his appointed role as forerunner, testifying: 'I saw the Spirit descend from heaven like a dove, and it remained on him' (JHN.1.32). Christ's descent into the waters sanctifies creation itself — the Fathers taught that Christ's baptism purified all water for sacramental use. The inclusion of a donor figure reflects the medieval understanding that through baptism, every Christian participates in this event. Paul develops the theology: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?' (ROM.6.3).

scripture_refs.reason: The full account of Christ's baptism with the Trinitarian revelation
John's testimony: I saw the Spirit descend like a dove and remain on Him
The heavens torn open, the Spirit descending, the Father's voice
Baptized into Christ's death, raised to walk in newness of life
Behold my servant in whom my soul delights — fulfilled at the baptism
